headlines.title: THE DAUGHTER OF BRAHMA
headlines.fullText: By I.A.R. WYLIE Author af THE NATIVE BORN, DIVIDING WATERS, Etc. Copyright, The Bokbe Merril Companys her crushed and helpless before these Erim realities of death and dishonor. Somewhere in the quiet house a door was banged sharply to. Hama Pal started and listened. His impassivity had returned and his oriental eloquence changed to a sharp business- The precision beherts of Intellect. Helleion, as much as drifted back to er in that moment, was to her a less suffering. Not for her were the consolations of religion nor the stole Toright thy husband attends a theatrical performance in aid of Indias charity," he aald in a low voice. "Thou wilt accompany him. I shall be was vain to hold out hands of suppli- close at hand. With thy handkerchief cation and death Itself. that last ref- they will give me a signal Tomorrow uge, opened out before her eyes an endless vista of future lives filled with same agony Ingen Even now her cmnd murs tainted with the dishonor of Its guten. had passed on into some foul body. tu begin the frig tru, se Nir. migrations. For it and for her Nir- and was lost and out of her numb despair there arose a are a free that had dragged her from her By evening we shall be on our way to Calcutta." He raised his hand in sol- shoot Sir David down like a dog thin met rest till may debt is paid. I swear it-thou knowest that I swear lessly to the door. "Nor think to be- ray me," he added. "Sir David is al- rudy marked, and when I fall a dozen will take my place. Tonight-or to- metrow The end will be the same." He was gone. She took a stumbling step toward the door as though to fol- low him, then swayed and subsided swb on her knees beside the cradle. move. The twilight deepened to celling and the red glow of the fire prake through the gathering Flotte gutt over the silken guilt that covered the burned up among the ashes of se mer its obiet, but called out theunknown bowers a passionate vin Mention of its own existence. Wronged. betrayed beyond all measure, she beat wildly against her invisible tauntera. seeking vengeance, above all, escape. She rose, shivering in every limb, and crept nearer to the fire. Escape? Was there none" In the days when evil dreams had risen before her had not she had the power to lift herself was left her and instead touched some- thing that was cold-cold and harder Yet another minute passed. Reall- mation, like a pale light breaking Then she started up. her hands search- meyin feverish milanes through the peace and silence? Her bewildered brain sought after the sacred form las, her lips formed them but no change came to hele The dreams age women who mocked and threatened darkness, closed her in, and suddenly. animal like some tortured frenzied animal, she screamed aloud. and stood still, arrested by the fright- ful nunihilating thing that she had touched. She did not cry out after the Her conception of life, half- childish. half-mystic as it was, left The door had opened. A flood of light fell upon her, and as though awakening from some awful trance, Med her standing in the doorway. He was in evening dress and his square broad-shouldered figure stood up mas- sively against the bright background. "I am here." "Sarasvati!" he repeated. "1 thought I heard you call. Are you there?" He switched on the red shaded lights face- over the mantel-plece and she saw his "I came to say good-bye," he said. "The carriage is waiting. I wish I hadn't to go, but I've promised. Good HEHt, my wife." "You are going- She passed her feeble hand over her forehead. "Tes- remember miamely alarmed. And merely The came toward him on tip- ter as in the days of her lost beauty. her fingers to her lips, a curious pain- ofthe grace- ful ausmer manche crept up to him and laid her hands upon his shoul. dert. "Take me with you," she whispered. Take me with you. I am so tired of the darkness-1 want the light-and music-and people-" He looked down ut her. She had spoken rapidly, me- chanically, and fever burned In her hollow cheeka. "The child-" he began. "Is it safe for you to leave him, dear lauth She laughed-a low tonelera little "Oh, he is so much better. He sleeps. I have sat and watched over him, and he has not chiesto the nurse can care for him. Let me He bent and kissed her tenderly with "As you wish it, you shall come," he It. baas suas ald Henceforth you must be always un meant 7 jate. We will put them out of our flate Me to world- ves and go back mme our own Yes, back into our own word, the for the last time we will go t Still he hesitated, oppressed by be new pot what. There was something But What was like a the Sea dank air, causing him keen physical discomfort. He looked hout him and his eyes encountered the white painted cradle. "Let us may good night to our son," Bald. tly .** But she clung to him with the same vtid laughter in her eyes Oh, no, you must not look at him ir touch him. Ite Is asleep. You must The doctor said so did he not? And pe sleeps so softly. Come-co gen- Ifurst allowed himself to be drawn "We shall send the nurse to him." She nodded and looked back over her shoulder. "Oh, yes-we shall send the nurse to husband-come quickly-come quick- She closed the door and ghostlike. gilded notselessly before him down the corridor. (TO BE CONTINUED.)

headlines.title: MEMORY OF UNION SOLDIERS HONORED
headlines.fullText: Memory of the Union soldiers who fought in the war of 1861-65 is per- ===== ======== wood cemetery that was unveiled with appropriate exercises Sunday. The shaft bears this Inscription: "Erected by the Woman's Relief Corps and Parmly Post No. 4. in Memory of the Union Soldiers of the Civil War. G. R. Members of the G .. A. Ir and the auto trucks leaving the courthouse at 3 p. m. The dedication address was delivered by Rev. F. E. Gordon of St. Paul's M. E. Church and the unvelling address by Ste matteo which Stands- for Which Our Monument Stands.". The service was conducted by I. W. Rouse, camp commander, and "Assem- Fly" and "Taps" were blown hy Erle Shue, bugler of Troop 1, Boy Scouts.

headlines.title: Between 500 and 600 Crowd Shores of Big Reservoir Sunday-Fishing Good.
headlines.fullText: It's hall Lake Worth, the new week- end summer resort and breathing sput. Between seo and suo menmuito mule children poured out by auto, wagon and street car, crowded the somis and rocks around the lake and lolled under the trees below the land about the lake and thronsed through the woods in every direction about the big dam. Society folks came from the autis Fons unloaded whole familles armed with fishing poles, lunch baskets and a happy-go-lucky idea that there must be fun and fish wherever so much water tumbled over the rocks. The more venturesome went bathing in the swift rapids below the dam and dozens of women and children waded played about in the shallow water. Everybody fished, at times, and about one man or woman in a hundred got a sek wen good catch of fish, but Immersed in water to their waists, some to thelr shoulders, all dodged the hot rays of the sun and looked happy in spite of red faces. The shore of the lake wan fringed with humanity that laughed at the hot sun and dug for balt. Children swarmed about the rocks and sands below the dam and in the swift runway several fishermen succeeded In getting the first good catches of perch and Sunday several quiet pools were found where swimming la compara- memed to enjoy the rushing shallower waters of the river immediately below the dam. The water han been fairly well drained from the lake and it is not very deep at any sont expert alone the old river channel. Bass have not begun biting very much and the white perch are caught occasiona ). atæed catfish and carp. The Makt i ative with emall carp and fishermen are advising that they be killed as fast as caught. go after bigger fish hit the lower rier at night- Above in the lake a few got some good catches of berch and catfish and gar fish weighing fifteen pounds, This was the occasion for an exhibition all during the day.
